title: US-Iran Ceasefire Framework Reshapes Region, Anti-Terror Draft Nears Finish, ISIL Sweep Nets 119
subhead: Washington and Tehran signed a 60-day ceasefire framework that could reopen Hormuz and redraw security calculations across the Gulf — with direct implications for Türkiye's energy costs and diplomatic positioning. At home, Ankara pushed forward its PKK dissolution bill, arrested 119 alleged ISIL operatives, and secured a Fitch rating affirmation despite inflation headwinds.

sections.heading: US-Iran Ceasefire Framework Signed
sections.body:
The United States and Iran signed a framework deal with a 60-day ceasefire on July 18, capping 108 days of military escalation that included US strikes on Iranian infrastructure and Iranian attacks on US bases across six Arab states. According to the New York Times and Reuters, the agreement commits both sides to a formal ceasefire while leaving major issues — including the scope of nuclear inspections, sanctions relief sequencing, and the status of Iranian proxy forces — for future negotiations.

US President Trump said the deal 'makes clear Tehran will never have a nuclear weapon' and vowed to release the full text within days, though an AP analysis found that White House talking points often did not match the agreement's actual terms. Iran's supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei called Trump's signature 'worthless' and warned of 'unforgettable lessons' if the deal is breached, according to Al Jazeera.

Even with the ceasefire, CENTCOM continued enforcement of a naval blockade on Iran in the Arabian Sea, and US strikes hit targets near Qeshm Island and Sirik in the early hours of July 19, reportedly in response to an Iranian attack that killed two US service members in Jordan. Iran simultaneously launched drone strikes on US bases in Kuwait, setting fires at Camp Udairi and Ali Al Salem Air Base, according to TRT World and Iranian state media.

The EU and Gulf Cooperation Council jointly called for Iran to permanently open the Strait of Hormuz without conditions or fees. Oil prices fell 5% to a three-month low on reopening hopes, though mine-clearing could delay full shipping resumption by 40-50 days, the Saudi Gazette reported. Brent crude stood at $88.10 per barrel.
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire is the first formal pause in the US-Iran conflict but leaves the hardest disputes unresolved. For Türkiye, Hormuz reopening would ease energy import costs and the current account pressure that Fitch flagged, but continued instability keeps insurance and freight premiums elevated.

sections.heading: Erdogan Claims Diplomatic Role as Region Reorders
sections.body:
President Erdoğan said Türkiye chose diplomacy over escalation throughout the Iran conflict, Daily Sabah reported, positioning Ankara as a constructive actor that avoided taking sides militarily while maintaining channels with all parties. The message was aimed at both domestic and Western audiences as the ceasefire reshapes regional alliances.

Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan is preparing to visit Moscow for Ukraine and Black Sea talks, according to Al-Monitor — a signal that Ankara intends to sustain its mediation portfolio even as the Iran file moves toward implementation. The visit comes as Zelensky seeks to revive diplomatic tracks at the G7 summit in France and the Black Sea security environment remains contested.

At the G7, European leaders pressed Trump on Ukraine strategy, while Macron and Zelensky were caught on camera game-planning how to handle the US president on the summit sidelines, Politico reported. The Iran deal has pushed Ukraine off the top of the G7 agenda, to Kyiv's frustration.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye's diplomatic positioning during the Iran conflict preserved relationships with both Washington and Tehran. Fidan's Moscow trip signals that Ankara wants to keep its mediation relevance alive — but the US-Iran ceasefire may reduce the demand for Turkish intercession just as it opens space for Türkiye to refocus on Black Sea and Ukraine dossiers.

sections.heading: Anti-Terror Draft Law Enters Final Stage
sections.body:
Türkiye's draft law providing the legal framework for PKK's dissolution and disarmament has entered its final stage, Hürriyet Daily News reported, citing officials familiar with the process. If a broad consensus is reached, the framework bill could be submitted to parliament's speaker's office next week and enacted by late July or early August, before the summer recess.

The legislation follows a series of meetings between AKP, DEM Party, and MHP officials. DEM Party lawmakers Pervin Buldan and Mithat Sancar met with AKP deputy chairman Efkan Ala on July 15, where AKP shared details of the Justice Ministry-coordinated draft. The DEM Party delegation later met MHP leader Devlet Bahçeli to discuss remaining unresolved elements.

A delegation is expected to visit İmralı prison island soon to brief jailed PKK leader Abdullah Öcalan on negotiations. The DEM Party has opposed categorizing PKK members by crime severity or seniority, while the AKP has insisted the proposal will not be interpreted as a general amnesty.
sections.whyItMatters: The speed of this process — potentially passing before August — signals that the government wants to lock in the PKK process before the Iran ceasefire reshapes regional Kurdish dynamics. If passed, it would be the most consequential legislative step toward ending the four-decade conflict, but the no-amnesty framing could limit its appeal to PKK elements.

sections.heading: ISIL Sweep Arrests 119 as Conflict Escalation Risk Persists
sections.body:
Türkiye's Interior Ministry announced on July 18 that authorities had arrested 119 people across 30 provinces accused of links to ISIL. The operation, which targeted Istanbul and Ankara among other cities, followed a June incident in which police killed an ISIL suspect in a gunfight south of Ankara, two weeks before the NATO summit in the capital.

Charges included ISIL group membership, propaganda dissemination on social media, and financing through intermediaries and 'so-called charities.' A separate earlier sweep detained 209 people suspected of links to ISIL or far-left groups.

The crackdown comes amid heightened regional instability. As the US-Iran conflict enters its ceasefire phase, Turkish security officials remain concerned about the potential for ISIL and other groups to exploit security vacuums in Syria and Iraq.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale of the sweep — 119 arrests across 30 provinces — signals that Turkish intelligence assesses ISIL's domestic network as more active than publicly acknowledged. With the Iran ceasefire potentially reshaping proxy dynamics across Syria and Iraq, Türkiye faces the risk of displaced or newly mobilized militants rather than a reduction in the terror threat.

sections.heading: Fitch Affirms Türkiye at BB- With Stable Outlook
sections.body:
Fitch Ratings affirmed Türkiye's long-term foreign-currency issuer default rating at BB- with a stable outlook, citing persistent inflation, limited external liquidity, and governance weaknesses as constraints — offset by low public debt, a diversified economy, and a resilient banking sector.

The agency forecasts inflation easing from 32% in June to 29.5% by end-2026 and 18% by end-2028, though it warned that elevated inflation expectations increase the risk that sharp policy easing could reignite macroeconomic pressures. Growth is projected to slow to 2.8% in 2026 before recovering to 4.4% in 2027.

Fitch noted that the Central Bank's recent increase in funding costs and tighter credit limits supported a partial reserve recovery after FX interventions during the early stages of the US-Iran conflict. Gross FX reserves are projected to reach $167 billion by end-2026. The current account deficit is expected to widen by 1.1 percentage points to 3% of GDP this year due to weaker energy and tourism balances.

An upgrade path would require sustained decline in external financing needs, stronger buffers, and greater confidence that tight policies will remain in place to support disinflation.
sections.whyItMatters: Fitch's affirmation is a holding pattern — not an upgrade Türkiye wants, but not a downgrade either. The Iran ceasefire could improve the tourism and energy outlook that Fitch flagged as current account drags. But with elections projected for late 2027, the agency's assumption of 'moderate pre-election stimulus' means fiscal discipline will be tested sooner than markets expect.

sections.heading: CHP Convention Feud Reaches High Court
sections.body:
An appeal against the court ruling that annulled the CHP's 38th ordinary convention — which saw Özgür Özel defeat Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu in the leadership race — has been referred to the Court of Appeals, Hürriyet Daily News reported. The regional court in Ankara transmitted the case nearly two months after the lower court declared the November 2023 vote 'absolutely void.'

After the annulment ruling, Kılıçdaroğlu's administration returned to office, dismissed the party's legal team, and withdrew the appeal. Özel subsequently filed a separate appeal in his personal capacity. The high court will first assess procedural requirements before considering the substance — including whether the lower court had authority to issue such a ruling and whether the alleged irregularities justified voiding the entire convention.

The court could also rule that the removal of Özel's administration was unlawful even if it agrees with the convention annulment, creating a complex split outcome.
sections.whyItMatters: The CHP remains paralyzed by internal divisions at a moment when the opposition should be capitalizing on economic strain and the PKK process debate. A prolonged court fight weakens the main opposition's ability to set an alternative agenda — which may suit the ruling coalition just fine.

sections.heading: Europe's Auto Industry Backs Türkiye for Made-in-EU Policy
sections.body:
Trade Minister Ömer Bolat announced after meetings in Brussels that the European Automobile Manufacturers' Association (ACEA) supports Türkiye's full and unconditional inclusion in the EU's 'Made in EU' policy framework.

Bolat met with ACEA director general Sigrid de Vries and attended a roundtable with member company representatives. The Turkish side argued that decades of European automaker investment in Türkiye demonstrated deep industrial integration through the Customs Union — and that excluding Türkiye from the 'Made in EU' framework would disrupt established supply chains.

Türkiye's automotive sector remained the country's leading export industry in 2025, with overseas sales rising 11.6% to a record $41.5 billion. Exports to EU countries totaled $30.11 billion, accounting for 72.5% of sector exports. Türkiye produced nearly 1.42 million motor vehicles in 2025, including about 872,500 passenger cars.
sections.whyItMatters: European automakers are effectively lobbying for Türkiye's inclusion because their own supply chains depend on it. This gives Ankara commercial leverage in the broader Customs Union modernization debate — but political headwinds in Brussels around rule-of-law and democratic standards continue to limit how far the EU is willing to go.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire framework is the most consequential regional development in months — but its implementation is fragile, with strikes and counterstrikes continuing even as the deal was signed. For Türkiye, the ceasefire eases the most acute energy and current account pressures (Fitch projects the current account deficit widening to 3% of GDP on the conflict's effects), but the remaining uncertainty keeps risk premia elevated. Domestically, Ankara is pressing ahead on the PKK dissolution law with real momentum, sweeping ISIL networks in a precautionary crackdown, and watching the CHP implode in court — all while positioning itself diplomatically for a post-ceasefire regional order where its mediation services may be in less demand but its economic stakes are higher than ever.
sources: Hürriyet Daily News, TRT World, Reuters, AP, New York Times, Al Jazeera, Daily Sabah, Al-Monitor, BBC, Fitch Ratings, Turkish Statistical Institute (TÜİK), Anadolu Agency, Saudi Gazette, Politico, CNBC, Bloomberg, The Economist, AFP, SpaceNews, Turkish Interior Ministry
